edges.quote: First to pay that price were the captive lords languishing in the dungeons under the Red Keep, men who had once sworn to defend the rights of Princess Rhaenyra and still stubbornly refused to bend the knee to King Aegon.
edges.note: Criston Cole's new hard line as Hand opens with the immediate execution of the captive black lords.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the executions are the very next narrated beat after Cole's 'steel fist' proclamation, with nothing decisional between the appointment and the killings.

edges.quote: “We will pay the princess back in her own bloody coin,” he told the king.
edges.note: As Hand and Lord Commander, Cole devises the covert-assassin mission that leads to the Cargyll duel.
edges.rationale: ENABLES not CAUSES: Cole's plan makes the fatal encounter possible, but it was chance ('the gods brought him face-to-face with Ser Erryk himself') that actually produced the duel, not Cole's design forcing it.

edges.quote: “You must rule the realm now, until your brother is strong enough to take the crown again,” the King’s Hand told Prince Aemond.
edges.note: Aegon's Rook's Rest maiming leaves him unable to rule, so Cole hands the regency to Aemond.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES targets the decision-event: Aegon's incapacitation is the reason, but Cole's proclamation is the human decision that actually installs Aemond as regent.

edges.quote: With Sunfyre wounded near Rook's Rest and unable to fly, and Tessarion with Prince Daeron in Oldtown, only two mature dragons remained to defend King’s Landing
edges.note: Sunfyre's Rook's Rest wounding leaves the greens dragon-poor, and Jacaerys reasons the blacks can now overwhelm Vhagar if he can field enough riders — the impetus for the Sowing.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: the battle's outcome drives Jacaerys's decision to call the dragonseeds, rather than mechanically forcing the Sowing itself.

edges.quote: In the end, the brown dragon was brought to heel by the cunning and persistence of a “small brown girl” of six-and-ten, who delivered him a freshly slaughtered sheep every morning, until Sheepstealer learned to accept and expect her.
edges.note: Nettles' taming of Sheepstealer is one strand of the wider Sowing call for dragonriders.
edges.rationale: SUB_BEAT_OF: Nettles' individual taming is one beat inside the larger named Sowing event, not an independent causal chain.

edges.quote: As Silverwing, Sheepstealer, Seasmoke, and Vermithor descended upon them, the men of the Triarchy felt their courage desert them.
edges.note: The four new dragonriders produced by the Sowing are what breaks the Triarchy fleet's line in the Gullet.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the Sowing supplies the extra dragons that make the naval rout possible; it does not by itself force the battle or its outcome.

edges.quote: The sight of the queen’s dragons in the sky above took the heart out of the opposition, and King Aegon’s remaining loyalists hid
edges.note: Sowing-produced dragonriders (Sheepstealer, Silverwing, Vermithor, Seasmoke) land on the city's hills as a show of force during the fall of King's Landing.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the extra dragons make the bloodless collapse of resistance possible, but the actual fall is forced by the City Watch's defection, a separate free decision.

edges.quote: Not long after Addam of Hull had proved himself by flying Seasmoke, Lord Corlys went so far as to petition Queen Rhaenyra to remove the taint of bastardy from him and his brother.
edges.note: Addam's Sowing success directly prompts Corlys's petition to legitimize him and Alyn.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the petition follows immediately ('not long after') from Addam mounting Seasmoke, with no other intervening event.

edges.quote: Finally one quarrel took him through the neck, and Jace was swallowed by the sea.
edges.note: Jacaerys dies in the water during the Gullet fighting after Vermax goes down.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: his death is the immediate next beat after Vermax's crash, within the same continuous action.

edges.quote: Spicetown was brutally sacked, the bodies of men, women, and children butchered in the streets and left as fodder for gulls and rats and carrion crows, its buildings burned. The town would never be rebuilt.
edges.note: Marquee Gullet consequence: Spicetown sacked and never rebuilt.
edges.rationale: Role edge wiring the location as a casualty of the battle hub, per the assignment's call to wire the Gullet's marquee consequences.

edges.quote: High Tide was put to the torch as well. All the treasures the Sea Snake had brought back from the east were consumed by fire, his servants cut down as they tried to flee the flames.
edges.note: Marquee Gullet consequence: High Tide burned and looted of the Sea Snake's treasures.
edges.rationale: Role edge wiring the location as a casualty of the battle hub, per the assignment's call to wire the Gullet's marquee consequences.

edges.quote: for the rank and file of the City Watch still loved Daemon Targaryen, the Prince of the City who had commanded them of old.
edges.note: The gold cloaks' lingering loyalty to Daemon drives their choice to imprison/kill Aegon's loyalist gate captains and open the city. [S204 adjudication: MOTIVATES->ENABLES — the Watch is the actor; its loyalty is the door-opener]
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: the Watch's disposition is what drives their free decision to betray Aegon and hand the city to Rhaenyra.

edges.quote: and ordered the city gates opened to the men pouring off the Sea Snake's ships.
edges.note: Luthor Largent kills Gwayne Hightower and, in the same breath, opens the gates to the blacks.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the gate-opening order is the same act, same sentence, immediately following Gwayne's killing.

edges.quote: Ser Otto Hightower, who had served three kings as Hand, was the first traitor to be beheaded.
edges.note: Once the city falls and Rhaenyra takes the throne, Otto is the first green lord executed for treason.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: Otto's execution is part of the immediate settling of accounts that follows directly on the city's capture.

edges.quote: Small wonder then that when word of the fall of King's Landing reached him, the prince felt thrice the fool. His fury was fearsome to behold.
edges.note: News of the city's fall enrages Aemond at Harrenhal, and he vents that fury on the Strongs, whom he brands traitors.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: 'First to suffer for it was Ser Simon Strong' follows in the very next sentence, with Aemond's fury as the direct, undeliberated spark.

edges.quote: Willow Pound-Stone tore the weeping prince from his arms.
edges.note: After Ser Rickard Thorne is shot down on the bridge, the mob seizes Prince Maelor from his arms.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: the boy's seizure by the mob is the immediate next beat after Thorne's death, in the same continuous scene.

edges.quote: Mushroom tells us that Willow Pound-Stone clutched the boy so tightly that she broke his back and crushed him to death.
edges.note: Disputed killing: Mushroom blames Willow Pound-Stone; Eustace omits her and blames a butcher; Munkun says the mob tore the boy apart and names no one.
edges.rationale: Dyad KILLS edge used sparingly per the vocabulary, explicitly to carry the disputed manner-of-death the assignment calls out; tier-2 + disputed + in_universe_source per the hard rules since only Mushroom names Willow as the direct killer.

edges.quote: In a voice as cold as ice, she commanded Ser Luthor Largent to take twenty gold cloaks to the Dragonpit and arrest Ser Addam Velaryon.
edges.note: Mysaria's accusation against Nettles hardens Rhaenyra's suspicion of the dragonseeds generally, and she orders Addam's arrest, which he flees.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES targets the decision-event: the accusation drives Rhaenyra's arrest order, and Addam's own choice to flee (once forewarned) is a further free decision, so TRIGGERS/CAUSES would collapse two layers of human agency.

edges.quote: Balked and angry, Ser Luthor returned at once to the Red Keep, where he burst into the Tower of the Hand and laid rough hands on the aged Lord Corlys, accusing him of treachery.
edges.note: Frustrated by Addam's escape, Luthor Largent immediately arrests Corlys instead.
edges.rationale: TRIGGERS: 'at once' signals the same unbroken scene, Luthor's reflexive redirection of his warrant onto Corlys.

edges.quote: Let him take her at table or abed and strike her head off. Only then shall
edges.note: Mysaria's accusation leads Rhaenyra to send a kill warrant for Nettles to Maidenpool.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: the kill order creates the threat that produces Nettles' flight, but it does not force it — Maester Norren's free decision to warn Daemon and Nettles is what actually produces the departure (see G17-E21).

edges.quote: found Prince Daemon and the girl Nettles at their supper that night, and showed them the queen's letter.
edges.note: Maester Norren chooses to warn Daemon and Nettles of the queen's kill order rather than deliver it silently, prompting their flight from Maidenpool the next dawn.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Norren's warning is the act that drives Daemon and Nettles' decision to depart at first light; it is his free choice against his own vows, not a mechanical trigger.

edges.quote: No longer tied to castle or host, the oneeyed prince was free to fly where he would.
edges.note: Aemond's unrestricted, solo raiding campaign is what eventually brings Vhagar within Alys Rivers's 'sight' of Harrenhal for the duel with Daemon.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: Aemond's freedom of movement is the precondition for the eventual confrontation, but he still has to choose to answer the challenge at Harrenhal.

edges.quote: You were a fool to come alone.
edges.note: With Nettles and Sheepstealer gone, Daemon is alone at Harrenhal with only Caraxes — exactly what lets Aemond risk the one-on-one duel.
edges.rationale: ENABLES: Nettles' departure is the precondition that leaves Daemon without a second dragon; the text has Aemond himself name Daemon's solitude as decisive ('You were a fool to come alone'), while Aemond's choice to actually attack remains his own free decision.

edges.quote: “Were | not alone, you would not have come,” said Daemon.
edges.note: Daemon's solitary vigil at Harrenhal was deliberate bait, by his own admission, to draw Aemond into single combat.
edges.rationale: MOTIVATES: Daemon's own words confirm his occupation and lone wait at Harrenhal was a calculated lure, driving Aemond's decision to come and fight rather than mechanically forcing it.

new_nodes.name: The Sowing (the Red Sowing)
new_nodes.identity: Prince Jacaerys Velaryon's call for any commoner or dragonseed who could master one of Dragonstone's masterless dragons, following the deaths at Rook's Rest; also called the Red Sowing or the Sowing of the Seeds. Produced four new dragonriders — Hugh Hammer (Vermithor), Ulf White (Silverwing), Addam of Hull (Seasmoke), and Nettles (Sheepstealer) — at heavy cost in dead and maimed seekers.

new_nodes.name: Death of Maelor Targaryen
new_nodes.identity: King Aegon II's younger son Maelor, fleeing with Ser Rickard Thorne, is seized by a mob at Bitterbridge after Thorne is shot down on the bridge; chroniclers disagree on the manner of his death (crushed by Willow Pound-Stone per Mushroom, hacked apart by a butcher per Eustace, or torn limb from limb by the mob per Munkun).

new_nodes.name: Mysaria's Accusation Against Nettles
new_nodes.identity: Summoned by a suspicious Queen Rhaenyra, Mysaria tells the black council that Nettles has taken Prince Daemon to her bed and carries his bastard, tipping the queen's wrath into arrest and kill orders against the dragonseed riders and Lord Corlys.

flags: Row 212 slug_candidates included criston-cole-appointed-to-kingsguard / criston-cole-named-lord-commander-of-the-kingsguard, but that appointment predates this section (17-p01 only narrates the Hand dismissal). Left untouched.

Verified battle-above-the-gods-eye vs battle-beneath-the-gods-eye are unrelated events (the latter is Maegor-era, 43 AC, Aegon the Uncrowned's rebellion) — only battle-above-the-gods-eye is in scope here.

'Battle of Tumbleton' node is a wiki-redirect stub (same_as: first-battle-of-tumbleton); all Tumbleton-related content in this section already lives on first-battle-of-tumbleton, which already carries a PART_OF dance-of-the-dragons edge, so no new wiring was needed there.

All event.battle nodes touched by this section (battle-of-the-burning-mill, sack-of-duskendale, taking-of-stone-hedge, battle-above-the-gods-eye, battle-at-the-red-fork, battle-of-the-gullet, battle-of-the-honeywine, first-battle-of-tumbleton, sack-of-bitterbridge, battle-at-rooks-rest, battle-by-the-lakeshore, butchers-ball, fall-of-kings-landing) already carry PART_OF dance-of-the-dragons edges from a prior infobox-merge pass — no gaps found for item 2 of the assignment.

harvest.note: Grand Maester Greydon on the Triarchy's High Council: 'thirty-three horses, each pulling in his own direction' — vivid characterization, homeless quote.

Hugh Hammer prophecy: 'When the hammer shall fall upon the dragon, a new king shall arise, and none shall stand before him' — unattributed origin, flagged as mysterious by Gyldayn; possible theory-layer relevance.
Celtigar's tax regime detail (silver stag per inn bed, tripled port fees, 'cunt tax'/'tail tax' smallfolk gossip) — rich hospitality/economics texture not yet extracted.
Septon Eustace's physical description of Nettles (crooked teeth, nose scarred from a slitting-for-thieving punishment) — first-class physical-description extraction target per project values.
